title: AI-driven bubble, NBFI risks key threats to global financial stability: RBI
description: The Reserve Bank of India’s (RBI) Financial Stability Report for June has flagged a sharp build-up of risk around artificial intelligence (AI)-linked investments globally, even as it assessed that India’s domestic financial system remains resilient despite the lingering effects of the West Asia conflict. The report notes that global financial markets, having recovered from the initial shock of the West Asia conflict, are now being propped up largely by enthusiasm around AI. This, even as a combination of softer-than-expected oil price increases, strong corporate earnings, the AI-driven rally and supportive financial conditions has kept volatility “contained”.However, the RBI warns this optimism conceals mounting concentration risk as a small cluster of AI-linked firms is increasingly driving stock market performance in economies at the centre of AI adoption, with just five US stocks contributing roughly half of the S&P 500 returns this year; similarly, just two companies have done so in Korea and Taiwan. It adds that even the “outperformance” in some emerging markets was similarly driven by a handful of AI-linked companies and not broad-based growth.While emphasising that this remains a source of “financial fragility”, the report adds that “sell-offs in these firms could cause broader market declines in the US, and cause spillovers to other markets through wealth effects.”The report highlights that AI investment is now spilling into bond markets. Hyperscalers, or massive cloud service companies, have ramped up capital expenditure on AI infrastructure even as their free cash flows have declined. For instance, companies including Microsoft, Meta, Google, Nvidia, Amazon and Anthropic have sharply increased debt issuances over the past two years to fund this buildout, with longer-tenor borrowing rising notably in 2026. The report warns that an AI-driven asset price correction could pose systemic risks, since banks may carry indirect exposure through private credit firms and other intermediaries financing the boom.Also Read: Waiting for India’s big bang innovation in technologyNon-bank lenders amplifying riskThe report notes that as non-bank financial intermediaries (NBFIs) are growing at twice the pace of the banking sector globally, “their rising footprint across asset classes heightens the risk of shock amplification and turning market turmoil into financial instability”.Hedge funds’ exposure to sovereign bonds and interest rate derivatives has more than doubled from less than $9 trillion in 2020 to over $18 trillion in 2025, and their use of high leverage in arbitrage trades leaves them vulnerable to rapid unwinding during stress. This was seen in past episodes when forced treasury sales pushed yields higher. NBFIs are also dampening volatility in equity options markets through heavy trading in short-dated contracts, a dynamic the RBI says can itself amplify market swings during periods of stress, while leveraged ETFs add to mechanical price amplification.Also Read: Oil prices bring relief; monsoon, West Asia risks dim growth, inflation outlookBond market strains and sovereign debtBeyond AI-specific risks, the report flags broader bond market fragilities. Advanced economy government bond yields have surged to 20-year highs in some cases following the war in West Asia. “The rise in sovereign bond yields is being driven by multiple factors, including inflationary pressures from higher energy prices linked to the West Asia conflict, central bank rate hike expectations, sustained fiscal deficits necessitating greater bond issuance, higher term and inflation premia demanded by investors amid rising geopolitical uncertainty, and quantitative tightening by central banks,” the report explains.Compounding the strain, governments have also shortened the maturity profile of their debt in recent years, leaving them more exposed to refinancing risk just as borrowing needs are rising, refinancing needs among OECD countries hit a record $13.5 trillion in 2025, accounting for around 80 percent of total borrowing and is expected to rise further.India’s buffers hold, but funding pressures emergeOn the domestic front, the central bank struck a more reassuring tone. India’s banking sector continues to show strong capital adequacy, and declining non-performing assets. Foreign exchange reserves of $672.6 billion (as of June 19) comfortably cover external financing needs.Still, the report flags “funding is emerging as a key challenge”. Banks’ funding mix is shifting from low-cost current and savings deposits toward costlier term deposits, squeezing margins, even as credit demand stays robust. Foreign portfolio investors pulled a record $30.7 billion from Indian equities this year, the steepest outflow in two-and-a-half decades, pushing their ownership share in domestic equities to a two-decade low.Gold loans also in focusThe report separately highlights the rapid expansion of gold loans, growing at over 40 percent annually, driven largely by existing borrowers leveraging higher gold prices. Gold loans have not only emerged as the largest segment within non-housing retail loans, but have grown at twice the pace of overall non-housing retail loans. Moreover, banks and NBFCs have significantly expanded their gold loan portfolios in FY26, “outpacing growth in other retail loan categories, including housing loans”.

title: Oil prices bring relief; monsoon, West Asia risks dim growth, inflation outlook
description: Though progress in US-Iran negotiations have helped moderate global crude oil prices, providing some breathing room, an erratic monsoon and lingering tensions in West Asia pose risks for growth and inflation, says the latest monthly economic review by India’s Ministry of Finance.“While easing oil prices and improving global supply-chain conditions may help alleviate some external pressures, uncertainties surrounding the monsoon and geopolitical developments in West Asia continue to pose downside risks to the growth and upside risks to the inflation outlook,” the report notes. It adds that even as the economy continues to exhibit resilience, emerging signs of moderation in industrial activity along with evolving inflationary pressures “demand closer attention ahead”.Monsoon emerges as key concernThe most pressing concern flagged in the review is the weather, due to the heavy influence of emerging El Niño conditions over the tropical Pacific Ocean. The southwest monsoon is so far expected to be well below the long-period average (LPA), with kharif sowing running 22.7 percent behind last year’s pace. While healthy reservoir levels and ample fertiliser stocks offer some cushion, the ministry noted that the spatial unevenness of rainfall, with nearly three-quarters of meteorological subdivisions reporting deficient or large-deficient rain, remains a genuine threat to the agricultural outlook. The report notes that as of June 28, cumulative rainfall remains 43 percent below the long-term average.Also Read: Gold and Silver Prices Add Fresh Pressure to India’s Inflation Basket“Delayed onset and weak progress of the southwest monsoon have weighed on kharif sowing, and the evolution and spatial distribution of monsoon rainfall will remain critical for the agricultural outlook,” the report says. However, with combined wheat and rice stocks exceeding 4.4 times the prescribed buffer norm, foodgrain stocks remain “comfortable” and provide a cushion against potential supply-side disruptions.Global oil markets cool, but risks persistThe report states that global energy markets cooled considerably through June, as progress in US-Iran negotiations helped pull Brent crude down from levels above $120 a barrel at the height of the crisis in April to under $80 a barrel, averaging around $89.4 a barrel by late June. Yet the ministry cautioned that this correction remains “fragile”, given continuing disruptions to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz and the US Energy Information Administration’s recent upward revision of expected oil-production shut-ins in the region. “Global crude inventories are expected to remain under pressure in the near term, even as shipping and production conditions gradually normalise,” the report says.On the domestic front, the economy notched robust growth of 7.7 percent for 2025-26, with momentum carrying into the new fiscal year. Gross domestic product (GDP) growth in Q4 FY26 accelerated to 7.8 percent from 7 percent in Q4 FY25, powered by a sharp pickup in services (9.9 percent) and continued strength in industry (9.3 percent), alongside the strongest investment expansion recorded under the current GDP series.Also Read: India IIP Growth Slows to 4.1 Percent in March Amid West Asia WarHigh-frequency indicators such as e-way bill generation, PMI readings and electricity consumption suggest this momentum has largely held in FY27 so far, even as some indices, core industries output, fuel consumption, air travel and consumer confidence, point to a degree of cooling. “The recent de-escalation of geopolitical tensions in West Asia is expected to improve global risk sentiment, ease commodity price pressures, and support domestic economic activity in the months ahead,” the report notes.Fiscal position strengthensThe government’s finances also strengthened markedly. The Centre’s fiscal deficit narrowed to 4.4 percent of GDP in FY26, the lowest since 2018-19, aided by broad-based tax collections and a jump in non-tax revenue from higher Reserve Bank of India and public-sector dividends. Capital spending kept rising, up nearly 19 percent year-on-year in April, even as revenue expenditure held steady as a share of output.Current and capital flowsTrade performance was a particular bright spot, with combined merchandise and services exports growing nearly 16 percent year-on-year in May, and merchandise shipments hitting a record monthly high in May. The India-Oman trade pact and the upcoming India-UK agreement, along with expanded farm and marine market access to the European Union, are expected to sustain this momentum.Remittances, too, hit a record $155 billion for the year from $135.4 billion in FY25, defying fears that Gulf-related disruptions would dent inflows, a resilience the ministry attributes to remittances being driven more by overseas employment conditions than by financial-market sentiment. “This resilience is consistent with empirical findings that remittances are among the most stable components of external financing, remaining relatively insulated from episodes of financial market volatility and geopolitical uncertainty,” the report notes.Meanwhile, foreign investment told a more divided story. Foreign direct investment strengthened for a fourth straight month at $6.6 billion in April, but portfolio investors pulled out a combined $10.7 billion over April and May amid the regional risk-off mood, though early June data hint at a turnaround following the US-Iran agreement. The rupee, having weakened nearly 4 percent against the dollar since late February, has begun to stabilise as tensions ease.

title: WhatsApp rolls out username feature; not all users are happy
description: On June 29, WhatsApp introduced a new feature that enables users to reserve usernames for themselves, similar to what Telegram, another messaging app, launched in 2014. The WhatsApp feature will be rolled out over the coming months, but users can reserve usernames of their choice from this week; they will be notified once the feature becomes available in their country. The app will provide a username generator to help users—there are 3 billion of globally—make a pick. The feature will help creators, small businesses and organisations, who need to maintain a consistent presence online, claim their existing Instagram or Facebook username on WhatsApp.The Meta-owned messaging app said in its blog post on June 29 that this feature is a step towards increased privacy for users: “Usernames are our latest step to make WhatsApp even more private. There's no directory to browse and no suggestions—people will need to know your exact username to contact you for the first time. To help control who can reach you on WhatsApp with your username, we've built an optional username key that others will need to know to message you.”Users can choose to enable the feature if they want to keep their phone numbers private. At present, anyone who has your phone number can contact you on WhatsApp, but if you have enabled the new feature, they will need to know your username to contact you and will not be able to see your phone number. If you have already saved the person’s phone number, you will continue to see the name under which you saved that number.Your username will appear across direct messages, group chats and calls, and will be displayed with an @ symbol to distinguish it from a display name or phone number. To make sharing easier, WhatsApp will allow users to generate a QR code or a shareable link that directs others to their username. The feature is available on mobile devices and not on WhatsApp Web or Desktop.According to WhatsApp, the username should be between three and 35 characters long, can contain lowercase letters, digits, and special characters like underscores and periods. It cannot be a string of numbers only, it must be unique and can’t contain restricted words or phrases. Users can create, change or delete their username at any time, however, there will be a limit to the number of changes; previous usernames will become available to others after 14 days. Usernames can be transferred to new phone numbers.Also Read: WhatsApp Lawsuit Explained: Privacy Claims, Message Access and India’s DPDP ActWhatsApp will also offer an optional username key, which, when enabled, will require the other person to use both the username and the key to contact you. Existing contacts, people who have already chatted with, members of shared groups, those who have scanned your QR code, or anyone the user has messaged first will not need the key. The key is generated by WhatsApp and can be changed, regenerated or removed at any time. Enabling the feature will not affect existing conversations.The username feature has been announced days after Kunal Shah was announced as the new global head of WhatsApp, as the company aims to monetise its presence in India, where it has 500 million users. The broader vision is to transform WhatsApp into a ‘super app’ offering a wider range of services.“Timing is everything. Joined WhatsApp early enough to claim my username before we release this to the world. Time to get yours. A more private way to connect. Coming soon to your WhatsApp,” wrote Shah on X.In response to Shah’s post, Telegram wrote on X, “October 2014, is that you?”, while adding a link to its blog post from 12 years ago when it had rolled out the same feature.Everyone, however, is not as excited about the new feature. Entrepreneur and author Ankur Warikoo raised a red flag, saying scammers and fraudsters can create usernames that appear familiar and deceive users. “In a country such as India, this could be a disaster, if the right anti-abuse systems are not set up by WhatsApp,” he wrote on X.Jasveer Singh, CEO of KnotDating, a matrimony site, wrote on X, “WhatsApp just launched usernames. My first thought wasn't privacy - it was scams”, while Vijay Shekhar Sharma, founder and CEO of Paytm, wrote, “Soon you will have verified username on WhatsApp, and then unverified similar-sounding usernames… which in turn will…”WhatsApp has not mentioned if and how the username feature will be integrated with WhatsApp Payments.

title: Protest: ଭାରତର ସହାୟତା ଲୋଡ଼ିବାକୁ ବିକ୍ଷୋଭକାରୀଙ୍କ ଚେତାବନୀ
description:
ଇସଲାମାବାଦ: ପାକିସ୍ତାନ ନିୟନ୍ତ୍ରଣରେ ଥିବା କାଶ୍ମୀର (ପିଓକେ)ର ରାୱାଲାକୋଟରେ ୨୨ ଦିନ ଧରି ଗଣବିକ୍ଷୋଭ ଜାରି ରହିଥିବା ବେଳେ, ଅତ୍ୟାବଶ୍ୟକ ସାମଗ୍ରୀ ଉପରୁ କଟକଣା ନହଟିଲେ ଭାରତର ସହାୟତା ଲୋଡ଼ିବାକୁ ଆନ୍ଦୋଳନକାରୀମାନେ ଇସଲାମାବାଦକୁ ଚେତାବନୀ ଦେଇଛନ୍ତି। ଭୀଷଣ ଅର୍ଥନୈତିକ ସଙ୍କଟ ଏବଂ ଇଣ୍ଟରନେଟ୍ ବ୍ଲାକଆଉଟ୍ ବିରୋଧରେ ସ୍ଥାନୀୟ ଲୋକେ ପାକିସ୍ତାନ ସେନା ଓ ପ୍ରଶାସନର ଏକଛତ୍ରବାଦୀ ଶାସନକୁ ପ୍ରତ୍ୟାଖ୍ୟାନ କରିଛନ୍ତି। ଆଜି ଆନ୍ଦୋଳନକାରୀମାନେ ପାକ୍‌ ପ୍ରଶାସନକୁ ଚେତାବନୀ ଦେଇ କହିଛନ୍ତି ଯେ, ପିଓକେ କଦାପି ପାକିସ୍ତାନର ଅଂଶ ନୁହେଁ ଏବଂ ପାକ୍‌ ସେନାର ଦମନ ନୀତିକୁ ଆଉ ବରଦାସ୍ତ କରାଯିବ ନାହିଁ।
ସୂଚନା ଅନୁଯାୟୀ, ଦୀର୍ଘ ଦିନ ଧରି ଚାଲିଥିବା ପ୍ରଶାସନିକ ଅବହେଳା, ଚରମ ଅର୍ଥନୈତିକ ସଙ୍କଟ, ମୁଦ୍ରାସ୍ଫୀତି ଏବଂ ସେ ଦମନଲୀଳା ବିରୋଧରେ ସ୍ଥାନୀୟ ଲୋକେ ରାସ୍ତାକୁ ଓହ୍ଲାଇଛନ୍ତି। ଜନଆନ୍ଦୋଳନକୁ ଦବାଇବା ପାଇଁ ଏବଂ ଏ ସଂକ୍ରାନ୍ତ ଖବରକୁ ଚାପିଦେବା ଉଦ୍ଦେଶ୍ୟରେ ଗତ ଜୁନ୍ ୫ ତାରିଖଠାରୁ ସମଗ୍ର ଅଞ୍ଚଳରେ ଇଣ୍ଟରନେଟ୍ ସେବା ସମ୍ପୂର୍ଣ୍ଣ ବନ୍ଦ କରିଦିଆଯାଇଛି। ଏହା ସହିତ ପାକିସ୍ତାନୀ ପ୍ରଶାସନ ପକ୍ଷରୁ ଗତ ଦୁଇ ସପ୍ତାହ ଧରି ପିଓକେକୁ ଖାଦ୍ୟ ସାମଗ୍ରୀ ଏବଂ ଅନ୍ୟାନ୍ୟ ଜରୁରୀକାଳୀନ ଜିନିଷପତ୍ର ଯୋଗାଣ ଉପରେ କଟକଣା ଲଗାଯାଇଛି, ଯାହାଫଳରେ ସେଠାରେ ସ୍ଥିତି ଅସମ୍ଭାଳ ହୋଇପଡ଼ିଛି। ଜଣାଶୁଣା ମାନବାଧିକାର କର୍ମୀ ସର୍ଦ୍ଦାର ଅମନ ଖାନଙ୍କ ନେତୃତ୍ୱରେ ଚାଲିଥିବା ଏହି ଆନ୍ଦୋଳନ ଏବେ ଉଗ୍ର ରୂପ ନେଇଛି।
ଜୁନ୍ ୯ ତାରିଖଠାରୁ ନିୟନ୍ତ୍ରଣ ରେଖା(ଏଲ୍‌ଓସି) ନିକଟରେ ଏକ ସ୍ୱତନ୍ତ୍ର ଗଣଧାରଣା ମଧ୍ୟ ଜାରି ରହିଛି। ସେଠାରେ ସମବେତ ଜନତାଙ୍କୁ ସମ୍ବୋଧିତ କରି ଅମନ ଖାନ କହିଛନ୍ତି ଯେ, ପାକିସ୍ତାନ-ପ୍ରଶାସିତ କାଶ୍ମୀର କଦାପି ପାକିସ୍ତାନର ଅଂଶ ନୁହେଁ। ପାକିସ୍ତାନ ଉପରେ ସେମାନେ ନିର୍ଭର କରିବା ଅପେକ୍ଷା ପାକିସ୍ତାନ ସେମାନଙ୍କ ଉପରେ ଅଧିକ ନିର୍ଭରଶୀଳ। ସୋସିଆଲ ମିଡିଆରେ ଭାଇରାଲ ହେଉଥିବା ବିଭିନ୍ନ ଭିଡିଓରେ ସ୍ଥାନୀୟ ବାସିନ୍ଦାମାନେ ପାକିସ୍ତାନୀ ସେନାମୁଖ୍ୟ ଜେନେରାଲ ଅସୀମ ମୁନିରଙ୍କୁ ତୀବ୍ର ବିରୋଧ କରିବା ସହ ସେନା ନିୟନ୍ତ୍ରିତ କୌଣସି ଏକଛତ୍ରବାଦୀ ଶାସନକୁ ବରଦାସ୍ତ କରାଯିବ ନାହିଁ ବୋଲି ରୋକଠୋକ୍ ଶୁଣାଇ ଦେଇଛନ୍ତି।
ଅମନ ଖାନ ଚେତାବନୀ ଦେଇଛନ୍ତି ଯେ, ଯଦି ପାକିସ୍ତାନ ପ୍ରଶାସନ ତୁରନ୍ତ ଅତ୍ୟାବଶ୍ୟକ ସାମଗ୍ରୀ ଉପରୁ କଟକଣା ନହଟାଏ, ତେବେ ଲୋକେ ଭାରତ ପାଖରୁ ସାହାଯ୍ୟ ମାଗିବେ। ଏହା ଦ୍ୱାରା ଅଞ୍ଚଳର ରାଜନୈତିକ ସମୀକରଣ ବଦଳିଯିବ ଏବଂ ଇସଲାମାବାଦ ଉପରେ ଚାପ ବଢ଼ିବ। ସୂଚନାଯୋଗ୍ୟ, ଏହି ଆନ୍ଦୋଳନ ଏବେ କେବଳ ପିଓକେ ମଧ୍ୟରେ ସୀମିତ ନାହିଁ। ବିଦେଶରେ ରହୁଥିବା କାଶ୍ମୀରୀ ପ୍ରବାସୀମାନେ ମଧ୍ୟ ବିଭିନ୍ନ ଦେଶରେ ଥିବା ପାକିସ୍ତାନୀ ଦୂତାବାସ ସମ୍ମୁଖରେ ବିକ୍ଷୋଭ ପ୍ରଦର୍ଶନ କରୁଛନ୍ତି। ମୁଜାଫରାବାଦ ସମେତ ସମଗ୍ର ଅଞ୍ଚଳରେ ସେମାନଙ୍କର ରାଜନୈତିକ ଉଦ୍ଦେଶ୍ୟ ପୂରଣ ନହେବା ପର୍ଯ୍ୟନ୍ତ ଏହି ଲଢ଼େଇ ଜାରି ରହିବ ବୋଲି ଆନ୍ଦୋଳନର ନେତୃତ୍ୱ ନେଉଥିବା ନେତାମାନେ ଦୃଢ଼ୋକ୍ତି ପ୍ରକାଶ କରିଛନ୍ତି।
Pakistan: ଅନ୍ତର୍ଜାତୀୟ ସ୍ତରରେ ଇସଲାମାବାଦ ପୁଣି ଲଜ୍ଜିତ: ପାକିସ୍ତାନରେ ପ୍ରେସ୍‌ ସ୍ବାଧୀନତା ନାହିଁ: ଭାନ୍ସ

title: LeBron James to leave Lakers to play 24th NBA career
description: LeBron James, the NBA's all-time leading scorer, will return for an unprecedented 24th season but not with the Los Angeles Lakers."To be able to thrive in that role for that period of time and then have to step back into the role that I’ve been accustomed with over my career, over my life, playing a sport and being able to thrive under that, and then just my teammates allowing me to lead them under extreme circumstances, that was pretty cool for me at this stage in my career," James said.
content: NBA icon LeBron James is reportedly poised to play a record-extending 24th season, but not with the Los Angeles Lakers (Allen Berezovsky)LeBron James, the NBA's all-time leading scorer, will return for an unprecedented 24th season but not with the Los Angeles Lakers.Lakers owner Jeanie Buss thanked James in a statement for his eight years with the club, confirming multiple media reports that the 41-year-old superstar had told the team he was leaving."LeBron James is one of the greatest athletes in history," Buss wrote in a statement posted on the Lakers' X account."We will always be thankful for his eight years with the Lakers -- including the title he led us to in 2020 under the toughest imaginable circumstances and the countless records he broke in purple and gold."James's agent and close friend Rich Paul had told ESPN that James opted to inform the Lakers of he planned to sign with another club before the Tuesday night start of the free agency negotiating period."King James" became a free agent after the Lakers' second-round playoff exit but had not yet confirmed that he would indeed continue one of the most extraordinary careers in league history, with four championships and an unmatched collection of individual records.As the start of the free agency negotiating period approached James had been linked with a possible return to Cleveland or Miami -- both clubs he led to titles -- or with a move to join his close friend Stephen Curry on the Golden State Warriors.James's unprecedented 23rd NBA campaign got off to a rocky start last year as he missed the first month of the season because of sciatica.Once fit he again proved remarkably effective, shooting 51.5% from the field as he averaged 20.9 points, 6.1 rebounds and 7.2 assists in the regular season.In the playoffs he averaged 23.2 points, 6.7 rebounds and 7.3 assists and led the Lakers to a first-round series victory over the Houston Rockets despite the absence of star teammate Luka Doncic, who was sidelined with a hamstring strain.James had helped push the Lakers to the fourth seed in the Western Conference, despite late-season injuries to Doncic and key contributor Austin Reaves.After beating the Rockets the Lakers fell to the defending champion Oklahoma City Thunder in the second round of the playoffs.James said in the wake of that exit that he was proud of a season in which he was "put into positions I never played in my career."- 'Extreme circumstances' -That included a reduced role alongside Doncic before the Slovenian's injury put him back at the forefront."To be able to thrive in that role for that period of time and then have to step back into the role that I’ve been accustomed with over my career, over my life, playing a sport and being able to thrive under that, and then just my teammates allowing me to lead them under extreme circumstances, that was pretty cool for me at this stage in my career," James said.James's tenure with the Lakers included the 2020 NBA title, in a post-season played in a Covid "bubble," and a victory in the inaugural NBA in-season tournament.It was with the Lakers that he broke the league's all-time scoring record, surpasssing Kareem Abdul-Jabbar's previous mark of 38,387 points in a game on February 7, 2023.He also realized his long-cherished dream of playing alongside his son, Bronny James, after the Lakers selected him in the second round of the 2024 NBA draft."We wish him all the best in the future, both on the court and off," Buss said. "He will always be a cherished part of the Lakers family."bb/lp

title: జూలై 1న ఎన్టీఆర్ భరోసా పెన్షన్ల పంపిణీ
description: NTR Bharosa Pensions: ఆంధ్రప్రదేశ్ ప్రభుత్వం అత్యంత ప్రతిష్టాత్మకంగా చేపట్టిన ‘ఎన్టీఆర్ భరోసా’ సామాజిక పెన్షన్ల పంపిణీ కార్యక్రమానికి నెల్లూరు జిల్లా సర్వసన్నద్ధమైంది. జూలై 1వ తేదీ...
content:
NTR Bharosa Pensions: ఆంధ్రప్రదేశ్ ప్రభుత్వం అత్యంత ప్రతిష్టాత్మకంగా చేపట్టిన ‘ఎన్టీఆర్ భరోసా’ సామాజిక పెన్షన్ల పంపిణీ కార్యక్రమానికి నెల్లూరు జిల్లా సర్వసన్నద్ధమైంది. జూలై 1వ తేదీ బుధవారం ఉదయం నుంచే జిల్లావ్యాప్తంగా లబ్ధిదారుల ఇళ్ల వద్దే పెన్షన్ల పంపిణీ ప్రక్రియ ప్రారంభం కానుందని నెల్లూరు జిల్లా కలెక్టర్ హిమాన్షు శుక్లా ఐఏఎస్ ఒక అధికారిక ప్రకటనలో వెల్లడించారు. ఈ మెగా పెన్షన్ల పంపిణీ ఉత్సవంలో భాగంగా రాష్ట్ర ముఖ్యమంత్రి నారా చంద్రబాబు నాయుడు స్వయంగా నెల్లూరు జిల్లా చిల్లకూరు మండల పరిధిలోని పున్నపువారిపాలెం గ్రామంలో క్షేత్రస్థాయిలో పాల్గొని లబ్ధిదారులకు నేరుగా పెన్షన్ సొమ్మును అందజేయనున్నారు. ముఖ్యమంత్రి రాకను పురస్కరించుకుని జిల్లా యంత్రాంగం ఇప్పటికే పున్నపువారిపాలెంలో అన్ని ఏర్పాట్లను పూర్తి చేసింది.



ఈ భారీ సామాజిక భద్రతా కార్యక్రమం నిర్వహణపై జిల్లా గ్రామీణాభివృద్ధి సంస్థ (DRDA) ప్రాజెక్ట్ డైరెక్టర్ వి. నాగరాజకుమారి సమగ్ర సమాచారాన్ని విడుదల చేశారు. జూలై 1వ తేదీ బుధవారం తెల్లవారుజామున 6:30 గంటల నుంచే పెన్షన్ల పంపిణీకి సంబంధించిన అధికారిక మొబైల్ యాప్ సిబ్బందికి పూర్తి స్థాయిలో అందుబాటులోకి వస్తుందని ఆమె స్పష్టం చేశారు. నెల్లూరు జిల్లావ్యాప్తంగా మొత్తం 2,86,663 మంది అర్హులైన పింఛనుదారులకు రూ. 126,24,39,500 (నూట ఇరవై ఆరు కోట్ల ఇరవై నాలుగు లక్షల ముప్పై తొమ్మిది వేల ఐదు వందల రూపాయలు) నిధులను కేటాయించినట్లు తెలిపారు. సుప్రీం కోర్టు మరియు ఎన్నికల సంఘం మార్గదర్శకాలకు అనుగుణంగా ప్రతీ పింఛనుదారుని ఇంటివద్దకే వెళ్లి బయోమెట్రిక్ లేదా ఐరిస్ విధానంలో ఈ నగదును పారదర్శకంగా అందజేస్తామన్నారు.



జిల్లా పరిధిలో ఈ పంపిణీ ప్రక్రియ ఎలాంటి అంతరాయం లేకుండా సజావుగా సాగేందుకు గాను మొత్తం 13,265 క్లస్టర్లను ఏర్పాటు చేసినట్లు పీడీ నాగరాజకుమారి వివరించారు. ఈ క్లస్టర్ల పరిధిలో పెన్షన్లు పంచేందుకు వీలుగా 6,593 మంది ప్రభుత్వ సిబ్బందిని గ్రామ మరియు వార్డు సచివాలయాల వారీగా ప్రత్యేక విధుల్లో నియమించారు. ప్రతీ సచివాలయం పరిధిలో పంపిణీ సరళిని పర్యవేక్షించేందుకు వీలుగా రూట్ ఆఫీసర్లను, ప్రత్యేక నిఘా అధికారులను కూడా నియమించినట్లు పేర్కొన్నారు. వృద్ధులు, వికలాంగులు, వితంతువులు మరియు దీర్ఘకాలిక వ్యాధులతో బాధపడుతున్న అభాగ్యులకు ఎండ తీవ్రత పెరగక ముందే, ఉదయం వేళల్లోనే పెన్షన్ సొమ్ము అందేలా పంపిణీ సిబ్బందికి స్పష్టమైన ఆదేశాలు జారీ చేశామని, ముఖ్యమంత్రి పర్యటించే చిల్లకూరు మండలంలో నిరంతర నిఘా ఏర్పాటు చేసినట్లు జిల్లా యంత్రాంగం స్పష్టం చేసింది.

title: Tyson Fury announces fight in Thailand as final Anthony Joshua warm-up
description: Fury will face Polish veteran Mariusz Wach in Thailand on the same weekend Joshua fights Kristian Prenga
content: Tyson Fury has announced details of his final tune-up fight before facing Anthony Joshua, revealing a clash against veteran Polish heavyweight Mariusz Wach in Pattaya, Thailand.The bout, to be shown on Netflix, will take place on Friday July 24, which is just 24 hours before Joshua takes on unknown Albanian Kristian Prenga in his own warm-up fight in Jeddah, Saudi Arabia.Should everything go to plan in those two contests, boxing fans will finally get the long-awaited showdown between the two best British heavyweight boxers of their generation when Fury and AJ face off in a blockbuster event in the latter stages of 2026, having agreed terms earlier this year.Anthony Joshua awaits for Tyson Fury later this year (PA Wire)Wach does have more of a track record then Prenga and fought for a world heavyweight title against Wladimir Klitschko – impressing as he took the Ukrainian great the distance before losing a unanimous decision for the first defeat of his career.However, that fight was way back in 2012 and the 6ft 7in Pole is now 46 years old, having dropped from a 27-0 record before facing Klitschko to now being 39-13.Among the defeats was a unanimous decision loss to Dillian Whyte in 2019, a similar defeat to Fury’s cousin Hughie Fury in 2020 and a points loss to Frazer Clarke in 2023.Mariusz Wach fought Wladimir Klitschko way back in 2012 (Getty)He was also knocked out inside two rounds by rising star Moses Itauma in July 2024 and Tyson Fury’s performance will likely be judged against that benchmark.Fury has been training in Thailand for the last few months, making the decision to box at the Max Muay Thai Stadium a touch more explicable and he is expected to fly to Jeddah the following day to be ringside for Joshua v Prenga, after which the build-up to the Battle of Britain can truly begin.The 37-year-old ended a 16-month retirement in April with a points win over unheralded Russian Arslanbek Makhmudov at the Tottenham Hotspur Stadium, which was his first fight in the UK in more than three years.Fury returned to the ring in April (PA Wire)“This is a landmark year for me in boxing and I’m excited to be able to bring this event to Pattaya, a part of the world that is special to me,” said Fury, referencing his training in Thailand.“We’re coming together to put on a great show for the fans, while using the opportunity to give back to the local community, who have been so good to me every time I have visited.“At the same time, I’m fully focused on preparing for a tough opponent and making sure I’m the best version of Tyson Fury going into fight night. It’s the start of a huge second half of the year for me.”

title: Diabetes Diet: डायबिटीज में ये 5 फल बन सकते हैं ब्लड शुगर के दुश्मन! खाने से पहले जरूर जान लें ये बात
description: Diabetes Diet Tips: डायबिटीज के मरीजों के लिए सही फलों का चुनाव बेहद महत्वपूर्ण होता है।  आइए जानते हैं किन फलों का सेवन सीमित रखना चाहिए और किन फलों को बेझिझक डाइट में शामिल किया जा सकता है।
content:
Fruits That Help Control Blood Sugar:  आज के समय में डायबिटीज यानी हाई ब्लड शुगर की समस्या तेजी से बढ़ रही है। बदलती लाइफस्टाइल, अनहेल्दी खानपान, शारीरिक गतिविधियों की कमी और बढ़ता तनाव इसके प्रमुख कारण माने जाते हैं। ऐसे में डायबिटीज के मरीजों के लिए सही खानपान चुनना बेहद जरूरी हो जाता है।
फल हमारी डाइट का महत्वपूर्ण हिस्सा हैं और इनमें कई जरूरी विटामिन, मिनरल्स और एंटीऑक्सीडेंट्स पाए जाते हैं। लेकिन क्या आप जानते हैं कि हर फल डायबिटीज के मरीजों के लिए फायदेमंद नहीं होता?
कुछ फलों में नेचुरल शुगर इतनी अधिक होती है कि उनका ज्यादा सेवन ब्लड शुगर को तेजी से बढ़ा सकता है। आइए जानते हैं किन फलों का सेवन सीमित रखना चाहिए और किन फलों को बेझिझक डाइट में शामिल किया जा सकता है।
हर मीठा फल नहीं होता सुरक्षित! जानिए असली वजह
सभी फलों में प्राकृतिक रूप से फ्रुक्टोज (Fruit Sugar) मौजूद होता है, लेकिन इसकी मात्रा हर फल में अलग-अलग होती है। जिन फलों में फ्रुक्टोज अधिक होता है, उनका ज्यादा सेवन करने से ब्लड शुगर तेजी से बढ़ सकता है। इसलिए डायबिटीज के मरीजों को ऐसे फलों का सेवन सोच-समझकर और सीमित मात्रा में करना चाहिए।
ये 5 फल बढ़ा सकते हैं ब्लड शुगर, रखें खास सावधानी
अंगूर
अंगूर में विटामिन-सी और एंटीऑक्सीडेंट्स भरपूर होते हैं, लेकिन इनमें प्राकृतिक शुगर भी अपेक्षाकृत अधिक होती है। इसलिए इसका सेवन सीमित मात्रा में करें।
चेरी
चेरी स्वादिष्ट होने के साथ पोषक तत्वों से भरपूर होती है, लेकिन इसमें भी फ्रुक्टोज की मात्रा अपेक्षाकृत अधिक होती है।
अनानास
अनानास में विटामिन और एंजाइम्स होते हैं, लेकिन इसकी प्राकृतिक मिठास ब्लड शुगर को प्रभावित कर सकती है। इसलिए मात्रा का विशेष ध्यान रखें।
केला
पका हुआ केला कार्बोहाइड्रेट और प्राकृतिक शुगर से भरपूर होता है। इसलिए डायबिटीज के मरीजों को इसका सेवन सीमित मात्रा में करना चाहिए।
आम
आम विटामिन-ए और विटामिन-सी का बेहतरीन स्रोत है, लेकिन इसमें प्राकृतिक शुगर भी अधिक होती है। इसलिए एक बार में ज्यादा मात्रा खाने से बचें।
इन फलों का सेवन भी करें सीमित
शरीफा और लीची जैसे फलों में भी प्राकृतिक शुगर अपेक्षाकृत अधिक होती है। इसलिए डायबिटीज के मरीजों को इनका सेवन कम मात्रा में या डॉक्टर एवं डाइटिशियन की सलाह के अनुसार करना चाहिए।
डायबिटीज में कौन से फल हो सकते हैं बेहतर विकल्प?
सेब
सेब में फाइबर अच्छी मात्रा में होता है, जो ब्लड शुगर को धीरे-धीरे बढ़ने में मदद करता है।
नाशपाती
यह फाइबर से भरपूर होती है और लंबे समय तक पेट भरा रखने में मदद करती है।
संतरा
संतरा विटामिन-सी का अच्छा स्रोत है और संतुलित मात्रा में इसका सेवन किया जा सकता है।
अमरूद
अमरूद फाइबर और विटामिन-सी से भरपूर होता है, इसलिए यह बेहतर विकल्प माना जाता है।
कीवी
कीवी में विटामिन-सी, विटामिन-के, फाइबर, पोटेशियम और एंटीऑक्सीडेंट्स अच्छी मात्रा में पाए जाते हैं।
इसके अलावा सीमित मात्रा में अनार, स्ट्रॉबेरी और ब्लूबेरी जैसी बेरीज भी डाइट में शामिल की जा सकती हैं।
ये भी पढे़ं–Mango Kheer: गर्मियों में आम से बनाएं शाही मैंगो खीर, एक बार खाएंगे तो बार-बार बनाने का करेगा मन
फल खाते समय इन जरूरी बातों का रखें ध्यान

एक बार में लगभग 100 ग्राम या उससे कम फल खाने की कोशिश करें।
मुख्य भोजन के साथ फल खाने की बजाय अलग समय पर खाएं।
हमेशा ताजे फलों को प्राथमिकता दें।
जहां तक संभव हो, फलों को छिलके सहित खाएं ताकि फाइबर की मात्रा बनी रहे।
फलों का जूस पीने की बजाय पूरा फल खाएं, क्योंकि जूस में फाइबर कम होता है।
जैम और प्रोसेस्ड फ्रूट प्रोडक्ट्स से बचें, क्योंकि इनमें अतिरिक्त चीनी मिलाई जाती है।
अपनी उम्र, ब्लड शुगर लेवल और दवाओं के अनुसार डाइट तय करने के लिए डॉक्टर या योग्य डाइटिशियन से सलाह जरूर लें।

ध्यान दें: डायबिटीज में किसी भी फल को पूरी तरह &#8220;प्रतिबंधित&#8221; नहीं माना जाता। सही मात्रा (Portion Size), सही समय और व्यक्तिगत स्वास्थ्य स्थिति के अनुसार अधिकांश फलों का सेवन किया जा सकता है। इसलिए अपने डॉक्टर या डाइटिशियन की सलाह के अनुसार ही डाइट प्लान बनाएं।

title: ବରଗଡ଼ ଆସିବେ ଅମିତ୍ ଶାହା: ୨ଜି ଇଥାନଲ ପ୍ଲାଣ୍ଟର କରିବେ ଉଦଘାଟନ, ଗସ୍ତ ପୂର୍ବରୁ ପ୍ରଶାସନ ତତ୍ପର
description:
ବରଗଡ଼,୩୦।୬(ପ୍ରେମାନନ୍ଦ ଖମାରୀ)- ସ୍ଵରାଷ୍ଟ୍ର ମନ୍ତ୍ରୀ ଅମିତ୍ ଶାହା ଆସନ୍ତା ଜୁଲାଇ ମାସ ୪ତାରିଖ ବରଗଡ଼ ଗସ୍ତରେ ଆସିବାର କାର୍ଯ୍ୟକ୍ରମ ରହିଛି। ଏହି ଗସ୍ତରେ ଆସି ସେ ଭଟଲି ବ୍ଲକ ଅନ୍ତର୍ଗତ ବାଉଲସିଂହାରେ ନିର୍ମିତ ୨ଜି ଇଥାନଲ ପ୍ଲାଣ୍ଟ ଉଦଘାଟନ ସହିତ ସାଧାରଣ ସଭାରେ ଉଦବୋଧନ ଦେବେ। ତେବେ ଶାହାଙ୍କ ଗସ୍ତ ପୂର୍ବରୁ ପୋଲିସ୍, ପ୍ରଶାସନ୍ ତତ୍ପରତା ପରିଲକ୍ଷିତ ହୋଇଛି। ମଙ୍ଗଳବାର ଉତ୍ତରାଞ୍ଚଳ ଆଇଜି ହିମାଂଶୁ ଲାଲଙ୍କ ସମେତ ବରଗଡ଼ ଜିଲାପାଳ ଆଦିତ୍ୟ ଗୋୟଲ, ବରଗଡ଼ ଏସପି ରବିନ୍ଦ୍ରନାଥ […]
The post ବରଗଡ଼ ଆସିବେ ଅମିତ୍ ଶାହା: ୨ଜି ଇଥାନଲ ପ୍ଲାଣ୍ଟର କରିବେ ଉଦଘାଟନ, ଗସ୍ତ ପୂର୍ବରୁ ପ୍ରଶାସନ ତତ୍ପର appeared first on Dharitri Odia News.
content:
ବରଗଡ଼,୩୦।୬(ପ୍ରେମାନନ୍ଦ ଖମାରୀ)- ସ୍ଵରାଷ୍ଟ୍ର ମନ୍ତ୍ରୀ ଅମିତ୍ ଶାହା ଆସନ୍ତା ଜୁଲାଇ ମାସ ୪ତାରିଖ ବରଗଡ଼ ଗସ୍ତରେ ଆସିବାର କାର୍ଯ୍ୟକ୍ରମ ରହିଛି। ଏହି ଗସ୍ତରେ ଆସି ସେ ଭଟଲି ବ୍ଲକ ଅନ୍ତର୍ଗତ ବାଉଲସିଂହାରେ ନିର୍ମିତ ୨ଜି ଇଥାନଲ ପ୍ଲାଣ୍ଟ ଉଦଘାଟନ ସହିତ ସାଧାରଣ ସଭାରେ ଉଦବୋଧନ ଦେବେ। ତେବେ ଶାହାଙ୍କ ଗସ୍ତ ପୂର୍ବରୁ ପୋଲିସ୍, ପ୍ରଶାସନ୍ ତତ୍ପରତା ପରିଲକ୍ଷିତ ହୋଇଛି। ମଙ୍ଗଳବାର ଉତ୍ତରାଞ୍ଚଳ ଆଇଜି ହିମାଂଶୁ ଲାଲଙ୍କ ସମେତ ବରଗଡ଼ ଜିଲାପାଳ ଆଦିତ୍ୟ ଗୋୟଲ, ବରଗଡ଼ ଏସପି ରବିନ୍ଦ୍ରନାଥ ଶତପଥୀ, ସୋନପୁର ଏସପି ନାରାୟଣ ନାୟକ ପ୍ରମୁଖ ଇଥାନଲ ପ୍ଲାଣ୍ଟ ପହଞ୍ଚି ପାରିପାର୍ଶ୍ବିକ ସ୍ଥିତି ଅନୁଧ୍ୟାନ କରିଥିଲେ। ଏହା ସହିତ ସାଧାରଣ ସଭା ସକାଶେ ସ୍ଥାନ ନିରୂପଣ ପାଇଁ ବିଭିନ୍ନ ସ୍ଥାନ ବୁଲି ଦେଖିଥିଲେ।
The post ବରଗଡ଼ ଆସିବେ ଅମିତ୍ ଶାହା: ୨ଜି ଇଥାନଲ ପ୍ଲାଣ୍ଟର କରିବେ ଉଦଘାଟନ, ଗସ୍ତ ପୂର୍ବରୁ ପ୍ରଶାସନ ତତ୍ପର appeared first on Dharitri Odia News.

title: Why LeBron James' Lakers exit could be blessing in disguise as LA opens up cap space
description: LeBron James is leaving the Lakers, but it might not be a bad thing for Los Angeles. Here's where they stand as far as free agency spending, and what targets they can pursue.
content:
Why LeBron James' Lakers exit could be blessing in disguise as LA opens up cap space originally appeared on The Sporting News.
                Add The Sporting News as a Preferred Source by clicking here.LeBron James is the No.1 player in the 2026 free agency class. He's also one of the most gettable. LeBron will be playing for a new team this season, as tweeted by ESPN's Shams Charania on Wednesday afternoon. His decision completely changes the landscape for the Lakers this summer. While it could appear devastating at first to lose the 22-time All-Star at first glance, it may end up being a blessing in disguise.LeBron James' departure opens up significant cap space for the LakersThe Lakers had some big free agent decisions to make this summer. They've already come to terms on a new four-year, $185 million deal for Austin Reaves. Had they re-signed LeBron, they would have closed the door on using cap space in order to remake the team. Instead, the Lakers now find themselves with up to $52 million to spend. That gives them the ability to completely flip their roster. As an example, they've been rumored in talks with All-Star big man Jalen Duren. They can now go ahead and give him a competitive offer that the Pistons could balk at matching. Duren is far from the only option for the Lakers. They can be aggressive in trades, taking players straight into their cap without having to send matching salary back. Lakers fans should expect dramatic changes over the next few weeks. That is important for a number of reasons. While LeBron had a great year and was a top 25 player last season, his playmaking and shot creation was duplicative with a lot of what Reaves and Doncic do. He was an overqualified third banana who showed that he could still carry the team when those two were hurt. That was a luxury which the team didn't need when all three were healthy. What the Lakers needed more than LeBron were the type of 3-and-D role players that Doncic had during his 2024 Finals run with the Mavericks. Los Angeles ranked No. 20 on defense last season and No. 22 in 3-point attempt rate. That wasn't necessarily the style that JJ Redick wanted to play, but it was the one that he had to given the personnel that he had.That will be changing next year. The Lakers will get players to space the floor for Reaves and Doncic better while simultaneously supporting those two on defense.Lakers free agency targetsThe priority for the Lakers should now be to grab a better starting center than Deandre Ayton, who opted into his $8.1 million contract and will be back next season. Doncic needs a better lob threat and rim protector. Duren is a mediocre defender but does make sense as a vertical threat. The offensive pairing with Doncic would be deadly. Walker Kessler and Mitchell Robinson are other starting-level free agent centers the Lakers can now pursue. And there is always the possibility they acquire another starter via trade. Myles Turner could be available and makes a lot of sense. The Lakers also need players who can shoot and defend. Tobias Harris, John Collins, Dean Wade, Kelly Oubre, Kenrich Williams, Peyton Watson, Keon Ellis, and Quentin Grimes all fit that bill. Lastly, the Lakers must decide what to do with their own free agents. Rui Hachimura, Marcus Smart, Luke Kennard, and Jaxson Hayes all played key roles on the team last season. The Lakers will need to waive their cap holds in order to sign better replacements. Some or all could be gone. This is going to be a totally revamped Lakers team without LeBron. They won 53 games last season with a roster whose pieces didn't totally fit together. As great as LeBron is and as much success as he brought the franchise, they should be able to increase that total next season with a more balanced team.Doncic is a top-five player in the league and is an offensive engine unto himself. Reaves is capable of taking another leap with more offensive responsibility. With better defensive infrastructure, the Lakers should make it further in the playoffs than last season's disappointing sweep in the second round. This will mark the start of a team fully built around Luka, and the Lakers should be in championship contention sooner rather than later with him as their centerpiece.

title: Supreme Court upholds ban on transgender athletes competing in women's sports in West Virginia and Idaho
description: The Supreme Court also ruled that transgender bans in sports are not a violation of Title IX.
content: The Supreme Court on Tuesday upheld state laws in West Virginia and Idaho that ban transgender individuals from competing in women's sports. The Supreme Court released its decision on Tuesday. In addition to upholding those state laws, the Supreme Court unanimously ruled that transgender bans in sports are not a violation of Title IX.In its decision, the Supreme Court sought to answer whether  "schools may maintain women's and girls' sports teams for biological females" under Title IX and the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.The Supreme Court considered two cases when making its decision. The first one occurred in West Virginia in 2021. That year, the state enacted the Save Women's Sports Act, which "prohibits male students from playing on female teams." That particular law states that sex is determined by biology, or the sex an individual is assigned at birth. West Virginia was sued by an individual — identified as B.P.J. in the decision — who identifies as female and sought to participate on the girls' cross-country and track-and-field teams at her school. B.P.J. sued the state for alleged Title IX violations and possible violations of the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment. The other case occurred in Idaho, which in 2020 enacted the Fairness in Women's Sports Act, "which prohibits male students from participating on female teams." That law also states that sex is determined by biology. It involves another athlete — identified as Respondent Hecox in the decision — who identifies as female and wanted to compete on a women's club soccer team. Hecox was assigned male at birth. She eventually sued Idaho, alleging violations of the Equal Protection Clause. After considering both cases, the Court determined "that separate sports teams for biological males and biological females are reasonable given the inherent physical differences between the sexes." Additionally, the Court rejected claims by B.P.J. that West Virginia was violating Title IX. The Court noted, "While it is an unhappy occasion whenever a student who wants to play sports cannot do so, the Title IX regulations guarantee only 'equal athletic opportunity.'"Shortly after making that determination, the Supreme Court ruled that "West Virginia and Idaho did not violate the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment."The Court found that the laws being challenged as part of the lawsuits by B.P.J. and Hecox "do not classify based on gender identity or transgender status." The laws in question classify "instead on the basis of biological sex," the Court determined.  Because of that, the Court upheld the bans put in place by West Virginia and Idaho. The ruling has potentially wide-ranging consequences for transgender athletes looking to participate in sports. While West Virginia and Idaho were the only two states to have their bans upheld, 25 other states have enacted similar laws. The Supreme Court's decisions on both issues — Title IX and the Equal Protection Clause — set the precedent for those states to also have their transgender bans upheld if challenged in court. It also creates a more viable pathway for other states to enact similar laws banning transgender athletes from competing in women's sports.

title: Iran heads home after a heartbreaking World Cup knockout, but fans say players should be proud
description: The Iranian national team left North America on Tuesday, departing from its World Cup home in Mexico following a tournament marked by repeated disagreements with U.S. officials, flashes of athletic brilliance and, ultimately, disappointment over barely missing out on advancing beyond the group stage.  “I think even though they lost, it gave people a sense of hope,” said Mohammad Modarres, 38, who traveled from San Diego to bid the team farewell.  Watching from the lobby of their Tijuana hotel, the team erupted in celebration when Algeria took the lead in stoppage time.
content: TIJUANA, Mexico (AP) — The Iranian national team left North America on Tuesday, departing from its World Cup home in Mexico following a tournament marked by repeated disagreements with U.S. officials, flashes of athletic brilliance and, ultimately, disappointment over barely missing out on advancing beyond the group stage. The players return to a homeland still in the grips of an unresolved conflict with Israel and the United States. But their fans say they should be proud. “I think even though they lost, it gave people a sense of hope,” said Mohammad Modarres, 38, who traveled from San Diego to bid the team farewell. Heartbreak over missed chancesAfter its three group stage matches ended in draws, Iran's World Cup future depended on either Algeria or Austria winning their match on Saturday.Watching from the lobby of their Tijuana hotel, the team erupted in celebration when Algeria took the lead in stoppage time.“I've never seen a room explode like that,” said Kimia Ranjbar, 25, a lifelong fan of Team Melli who had driven down from the Los Angeles area. But minutes later, Austria tied the game again, leaving the lobby in dismayed silence. It was the last of many disappointments throughout the tournament, including when a late goal by Shoja Khalilzadeh gave Iran the lead in its last match, against Egypt, before being ruled offside.Facing challenging circumstancesDistractions abounded off the pitch before and during the tournament, beginning with questions over whether Team Melli would even be allowed to play in light of Iran's war with the U.S. and Israel. What followed was Iran's denied request to move its matches to Mexico, a relocation of its base camp from Arizona, and the U.S.'s refusal to grant visas to key members of the Iranian team's staff. The U.S. also rejected Iran's request to travel to the U.S. two days before its Los Angeles matches, though it relaxed some restrictions for Iran's last match.During a World Cup security briefing Monday, Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin told reporters that the U.S. had made several accommodations for Iran's travel and repeated assertions that many of the people Iran originally requested to travel with the team to the U.S. were associated with Iran's Revolutionary Guard. “I'm just glad they're done and they're not coming back," Mullin said, adding that he “might have sung a song or two or maybe even danced a happy dance.”FIFA did not respond to request for comment.In a statement to The Associated Press on Monday, the Iranian team said Mullin's remarks showed a lack of commitment to international law and the basic standards expected to host a global tournament.“The fact that he openly celebrates Iran’s elimination says far more about him than it does about our team. It reflects a level of pettiness that cannot even tolerate the presence of a football team competing on the world’s biggest stage,” said the team, which declined requests to interview players and staff.Before decamping Tuesday, the team thanked Mexico and Tijuana for their “kindness” but questioned its treatment at the tournament by the U.S.“What we experienced was a series of decisions, logistical arrangements, and circumstances that undermined the sense of fairness — an impression only reinforced by the events of the final matchday of our group,” the team said in a statement.Members of the Iranian diaspora were also divided about whether supporting the team showed tacit backing for Iran's theocratic government, which many of them oppose. Some wanted to keep politics and sports separate.“You don't see someone screaming at (U.S. soccer star) Christian Pulisic for something Trump does,” Modarres said.Though the team spoke out against its travel restrictions, it avoided commenting directly on the war. But it didn't shy away from spotlighting the victims of a deadly missile strike on an elementary school at the start of the conflict.Players wore pins with the number “168” when they first landed in Mexico, referencing the number of people, mostly children, killed in the attack, which was likely launched by the U.S. They left a note in the locker room at Los Angeles Stadium, calling for peace “among all nations” and with the hashtags #168 and #minab, the school’s name.Sherry Ghaemi, an Iranian living in Los Angeles, called their stand for the young victims “honorable.” New friendships are forgedAmid the strife, players tried to focus on the sport. There were high notes, like when goalkeeper Alireza Beiranvand made seven saves to hold Belgium to a scoreless draw, and when Ramin Rezaeian scored off the outside of his boot to equalize against New Zealand.“They’re going home not as losers, they’re going home as winners," said Ghaemi. “We’re proud of them.”Meeting some of the players was a thrill for Siavash Khosrowshahi, a 32-year-old Iranian American who drove from Los Angeles to Tijuana on Sunday, the day after the team was eliminated.“It’s been really tough and stressful,” Khosrowshahi said of the months since the U.S. and Israel started the war. There were times during the conflict when he couldn't reach his parents in Tehran — but not Sunday, when he called his mother from the hotel and surprised her by putting Beiranvand on the phone.“It’s a source of happiness for her,” he said. Iranians and Mexicans also deepened a bond, as Tijuana embraced the team throughout its visit. “Irán, hermano, ya eres Mexicano!" fans chanted in Spanish whenever they saw Team Melli: “Iran, brother, now you are Mexican!”“Iran is taking home the best of our country, and this city, which is the way in which outsiders are received,” said Arely Ramírez, a Tijuana resident who turned up at the team's hotel Sunday hoping to meet some of the players.The feeling was mutual. “We’re leaving Tijuana today, but but our heart stays here,” head coach Amir Ghalenoei said Tuesday before the Iranian team left for the airport.On Monday, many players still looked solemn as they passed their last hours in Mexico. A few signed final autographs and stood for photos with fans, their smiles more muted than the week before. Despite the disappointment, some supporters were already looking ahead. “This whole year has been bad events, bad luck after bad luck” for Iranians, said Ranjbar. But the AFC Asian Cup is six months away, a new chance for Team Melli, she said. “I'll be watching them play for that.”___Associated Press reporter Graham Dunbar in Geneva contributed.

title: ‘To Them It Was Like A Game’: UN Commission Chair Justice Muralidhar On How Israeli Soldiers Targeted Gaza's Children - Exclusive
description: The children of Gaza were not collateral damage, says the chair of the UN's Independent International Commission of Inquiry — they were the target. In an exclusive conversation with Times Now Digital, Justice S. Muralidhar lays out the evidence behind the Commission's latest report, "The Essence of Childhood Has Been Destroyed," and why he believes individual soldiers can still be put on trial.
content: When Justice S. Muralidhar, former Chief Justice of the Orissa High Court, is asked what he cannot forget from the months his team spent examining how Palestinian children died in Gaza, he does not reach first for the medical evidence or the satellite data. He reaches for the videos the soldiers shot of themselves."For me, it was seeing those videos and those clips and statements of Israeli soldiers actually mocking the toys of children," he says. "You're in an apartment which is already demolished, you're looking at belongings of Palestinian children and you're mocking those. You're hanging a teddy bear from the tank and you're pointing a gun at the teddy bear. There must be some depravity." He returns to the same word a moment later: "To me, it's a level of depravity which is inexcusable."Justice Muralidhar now chairs the United Nations Independent International Commission of Inquiry. Its latest report, "The Essence of Childhood Has Been Destroyed", released on June 23, finds that Israel deliberately targeted Palestinian children — conduct the Commission characterises as genocide, crimes against humanity and war crimes. Speaking to Times Now Digital, he set out, point by point, the case behind that finding."There can be no doubt that children are very specific targets."        The report describes two deliberate methods. The first is airstrikes using high-yield, wide-area explosives in densely populated areas. The second is the targeting of children's heads and upper bodies with quadcopters, drones and sniper rifles.Asked whether he was alleging that individual soldiers chose to kill children, Justice Muralidhar pointed to what the soldiers had said themselves. "To them it was like a game," he says, quoting accounts the Commission has cited in the report. The quadcopters, he explains, "are pre-fitted with thermal imaging cameras… and on the screen they're able to see the image, whether it's that of a child or of an adult, and they're using it because of its precision." One soldier, he says, described it as being "just like pressing a button and killing a Palestinian, sitting wherever we are".      The most harrowing illustrations are the most deliberate. "There is a 10-day-old breastfeeding baby, and while the mother is feeding the baby, the mother is left unharmed, the baby is shot through the head with a single gunshot," he says. "There's another instance where the adult is holding the child by the arm, the child is killed, the adult is unharmed. So there can be no doubt that children are very specific targets for the Israeli soldiers."What distinguishes a pattern from the actions of a few rogue soldiers, he argues, is the testimony of the doctors. "There could always be rogue soldiers, one or two here and there as an aberration. Is it a systematic pattern?" he asks. "We rely on evidence — first-hand accounts of doctors who've treated the babies, and they've seen a large number of babies brought in with these single-shot wounds to the head and to the neck. So we can see that there is a pattern to it."A third strand of evidence concerns “cube-shaped pellets” recovered from children's bodies. "We've shown photos of several babies with wounds to the upper part of the body," he says. The pellets, doctors explained to the Commission, were "specifically used to target children, because the minute the pellet enters the body, it just goes in and spreads, like a cluster bomb, and causes maximum damage to the internal organs." At a public hearing in Geneva, he recalls, some of the doctors broke down, telling the Commission they had "not seen this trend of extensive injuries to children at the most vital parts."Why the Commission calls it intent, not collateral damageFirst, the circumstances. "We also find that children are being targeted when children are doing routine activities like playing outside their house, just walking along a certain way, some of them running to get food supplies, when there was this distribution of food supplies," he says. The danger followed them as evacuation zones shifted. "Wherever the Palestinians were in Gaza to begin with, they've been given evacuation notices and systematically they've been pushed from the northern part of the strip to the southern portion and even in the southern portion to the Rafah border," he explains. "They have to walk very carefully in a certain line, because if they're even seen straying here and there, they can be shot — and children are unable to understand what is this so-called international yellow line. No child can be explained all of that. So a child is seen picking up firewood, a child is seen playing outside."      He draws the line at hostility. "This is not a child in any form of hostility. Let's say a child is throwing a stone, or the child is pointing a gun at a soldier — one can understand. But these children are doing such innocent, routine things and they are being shot at. One child is holding a white flag and the child is shot at. So we can see that children are not getting spared." His refrain: "How is a 10-day-old baby a threat to the Israeli state?"Second, the failure to adjust. "In any crowd, if you intensely subject that crowd to a bombing or an attack, you can be sure the children will be more affected than the other adults." Such attacks, he says, "started soon after 7th October 2023, so we can understand that was a trigger." But the response never changed: "If you are the state which is doing this reprisal and you find in the first two weeks that among those killed children are disproportionately high in number, you would definitely change your tactic — if your intention is not to affect the children." Instead, he says, "those intensive airstrikes continued."Third, and for him the most telling, is what the attacks targeted beyond the children themselves. "Children constitute the biological continuity of any group… You target children, you make sure that group does not continue." Nor, he says, did the attacks stop at the living: "They are not targeting just newborns and pregnant mothers, they are ensuring that neonatal facilities are destroyed. So even if it's a premature baby and you need incubating services, it's not available — the baby is bound to die." He points to rising maternal mortality, stillbirths and malnutrition: "It's a combination of several things that gives a clear idea that the intention is to not allow the Palestinian to continue as a group."He is careful to mark the limits of the finding. "We are an investigating body," he stresses. "We are not a judicial body. The standard which we look at is reasonable belief – if the materials reasonably indicate that this is what has happened, we state it as our findings." A criminal court, he notes, would set the bar higher: "The court which is trying these criminal offences of war crimes, genocide, may insist on proof beyond reasonable doubt." He is candid about the gap: "We are open to this possibility that some of our evidence, when it gets tested in the court, the court may not agree that this is sufficient proof, but this is not to say there is nothing at all." The Commission, he says, verifies "one source of evidence with multiple other sources… It is only then we arrive at a finding."The units have been named – and the evidence is ready to shareThe Commission has gone further than description. It has identified the specific military units it holds responsible. "We have named them in Para 356 of our report," he says. "We've listed out all the battalions, the regiments, all of them."That evidence has not yet been handed to the International Criminal Court, but Justice Muralidhar explains the sequence. "It is part of our mandate that we are of assistance to all judicial mechanisms," he says. "A request is to be made to this commission to share its evidence, which we then react to. We've already submitted this evidence at the request of South Africa to the ICJ." The Commission's standards, he says, are built for court: "Our evidence-gathering protocols and the rigour with which we analyse the evidence is aligned with that of the International Court of Justice and the International Criminal Court. We pick up only such evidence which will pass muster in those judicial proceedings."      Israel's silence — and an 18-page rebuttal it never sent the CommissionA recurring theme is Israel's refusal to engage, he says. The Commission issues public calls for submissions, sends draft reports to both the Palestinian Authority and the Israeli government at least a fortnight before finalisation, and issues a formal note verbale to Israel's Permanent Mission to the UN in Geneva. "We do it once during the inquiry, we do it once at the time of the draft report being sent. No response," he says. "So Israel just refuses to participate." Citizens are kept out of it too: "Israel has very specific instructions to its citizens not to engage with this commission… Israel's stand is that the entire UN is working to target Israel, so any UN mechanism is likewise tainted with this inherent bias." Access is denied as well: "If as a commission of inquiry, we decide tomorrow to go to Ramallah, we will be refused permission. If we decide to go to Gaza city or even Rafah, they will refuse."A note verbale sent to the United States this time, he adds, also went unanswered. Some Israeli civil-society groups, he notes, do cooperate.      Israel did, however, prepare an 18-page rebuttal to the report, which it shared with the press and did not send to the Commission. Its substance, Justice Muralidhar argues, is revealing for what it omits. "You would have noticed they do not deal with these videos posted by the Israeli soldiers themselves," he says. "They do not say it is morphed, it is fake — nothing. They do not deny the statements made by their own politicians, which we have quoted. And they do not tell us positively what is that evidence they have which we have not considered." He insists the door remains open: "What stakes do we have? We have nothing to wrongly make accusations against Israel state or Israel soldiers… Let them place it in the public domain. Let them place it before the world."On the Israel’s Hamas counter: "I need to correct this"To the charge that the Commission ignored Hamas's use of children as human shields and the hostages taken on October 7, 2023, Justice Muralidhar pushed back firmly. "I think this is the narrative in the media. So I need to correct this." The record, he says, is otherwise: "When the 7th October incident happened, this commission was one of the first to produce a report, and that report detailed the atrocities of Hamas vis-à-vis Israel's hostages." On June 15, 2026, he notes, the Commission submitted a further report to the Human Rights Council "on actions of non-state actors — by non-state actors, we mean the settlers in the West Bank, and as far as the Gaza Strip is concerned, we mean Hamas and its affiliate bodies… particularly the abuses of children."The narrower question of children used as soldiers and human shields, he says, has been deferred, not dropped: "We have taken the care to say in Para 4 that this is a topic we are not investigating right now. It does not mean that we have decided not to investigate it at all. And when we take up such a topic, we will also address the issue of settler children being used in the violence by arming them. So it will be across the board." He frames the Commission's purpose broadly: "This commission is interested in uncovering the truth… It could be Israeli citizens, it could be Palestinians, it could be others. We need to find ways of getting justice to all of them."The United States, the "Board of Peace," and a claim of improvementJustice Muralidhar is sharply critical of the US-backed "Board of Peace," set up as an alternative in October 2025. "It looked only at Gaza," he says. "If you see the peace plan, it is called the Donald J. Trump Peace Plan and it has been endorsed as such by the Security Council. It says there is an issue as far as Gaza is concerned. Gaza is not the only issue — there is West Bank, there is East Jerusalem left out." He calls the plan "ahistorical": "It forgets that there is something called a two-state solution, which was the very bedrock of the 1947 resolution of the UN," and "it ignores the fact that there are so many UN mechanisms actively involved in uncovering the truth of what is happening in occupied Palestinian territory." Membership, he notes, carries a price: "To be a member, you have to pay 1.5 billion dollars… Of the so-called 39 countries, they have pledged this 1.5 billion, but none of them has paid up."When the Board reported to the Security Council in May that conditions in Gaza had "vastly improved," he says, the claim did not hold. The exchange of hostages, he allows, "no one denies" — but "the entire Gaza Strip has not improved one bit. If not, it has become worse." Claims of large-scale aid, he argues, are also false: "The report also claimed that humanitarian aid is now entering in large measure into Gaza, which again is not true. We have just seen how three waves of flotilla activists have been stopped on territorial waters by Israel and how they have been mistreated."He recounts presenting to the Security Council, where 13 of 15 members were present — Russia and the United States did not participate. The reaction surprised him. "Some of them were really emotional and they felt disturbed. Then they asked, what can we do as Security Council? So the question itself was surprising — because what is it that they cannot do?" His answer was concrete: convene the body they had created — "Will you organize a meeting where we make our presentations to the Board of Peace?… Let it be cognizant of what the ground situation is" — and let the Organisation of Islamic countries call a meeting on the findings. Above all, he urged agreement on a floor of principle: "In times of conflict, no child should die of starvation." The report, he says, records that "more than 150 children have died of starvation." A second: "No child should die for want of medical help." His refrain throughout: "Children can't be made fodder. They can't be subject to geopolitical compulsions."      "Come back to this political compulsion… Many of them have trade arrangements. Many of them are dependent on the US for various things. So the US does control the decision of a large number of blocks of countries." The cost of collective silence, he warns, is moral as much as political: "It kills a bit of humanity in each of us."The killings that continued after the ceasefireOne of the report's most striking findings is that children continued to be killed after the October 2025 ceasefire. Asked whether that implicates the powers that brokered the ceasefire, he treats the question as rhetorical, then turns to enforcement. "Many countries are suppliers of arms to Israel. Those arms are being used to target children like this," he says. "Will those countries tolerate it if it was directed against their own children? They obviously will not."He draws on India's own experience to make the point. "Let me pick up the example of Kulbhushan Jadhav. When he was wrongly taken into custody by Pakistan, denied a fair trial, where did we go to? We went to the International Court of Justice. We got an order in our favour," he says. "If Pakistan had failed to implement it, we would have hauled up Pakistan." The contrast with Gaza is the crux of his argument: "That same International Court of Justice has given three provisional orders. Those orders are being violated with impunity by Israel. And what are our countries doing? As if it's happening to someone somewhere else." For him, that is "a clear frontal challenge to the efficacy of your international legal order."The human stakes, he insists, are not abstract. "You're growing up in a displacement camp today," he says. "We are playing with sewage water. There is no drinking water, there's no electricity. And 700 people are sharing one toilet. There are drones hovering all around, and the child is already mentally traumatised. What kind of a healthy future is this child going to see?"Why US backing emboldens Israel — and how a budget cut hampers the inquiryIsrael's confidence in not responding, he argues, is rooted in American support. "The support they have from the US does embolden them in a large way," he says. "It's not just financial support. It's also moral support. Somewhere US completely endorses every stand that Israel takes."He also points to a concrete consequence of the US withdrawing funding. "Because it has stopped contributing financially to the UN mechanism, 40% of its budget is now reduced," he says — work across many mandates, including his own, is "seriously hampered." The effect is practical: "In the initial phase of this commission from 2021 onwards, it could hold public hearings in the neighbouring countries – Jordan, Egypt, Turkey. But after the cut in the budget, we don't have the resources to even travel to those countries for holding public hearings."Who could still be put on trialAsked whether any of this will lead to a prosecution, Justice Muralidhar points to a development he finds genuinely promising. "The Hind Rajab Foundation have now instituted proceedings in several countries. It's a very interesting development," he says. "They have picked up on the fact that in one battalion, there could be soldiers of other nationalities who are serving. To be precise, there are more than 700 Australian citizens serving in the Israeli Defence Forces. There are more than 12,000 US citizens." His point is jurisdictional: "When these individuals go back to their respective countries, they can be investigated for their role in these atrocities, and they can be prosecuted by those courts, by exercise of what we call universal jurisdiction", because, he adds, those states "are themselves parties to so many treaties and conventions, and they have an obligation in international law to ensure that those treaty provisions are enforced."         https://youtu.be/weumu4gQx38?si=O3jkz-4UGvHHqMIxSome countries, he says, "have reached out to our commission to share the evidence. So that's already happening." "If they're taken seriously by member nations, they need not wait for a green signal from the International Court of Justice or the International Criminal Court, or because the US will be upset or somebody else will be upset," he says. "If they're serious about the international legal order and enforcing all these mechanisms, they can take action." The Commission's evidence, he stresses, is "gathering in the first phase for preparation of a charge. You can test this evidence. You can find more things. And you can take it to its logical conclusion."He is clear that Gaza is not the only conflict deserving scrutiny – Sudan among them – and that the UN runs many parallel mechanisms. "We are not the only commission of inquiry," he says. "There are several other independent commissions of inquiry, there are fact-finding missions, there are independent experts, there are special rapporteurs."One message, and one boyAsked for a single message to Israel, his answer is brief: "Cooperate with all other countries, including the UN, in restoring faith in humanity.""This is a message to everyone. It's not just to the US or to Israel," he says.He ends by reading from the testimony of a boy  whose mother and two sisters were killed in airstrikes. "He saw them die in front of his eyes. Such a boy — he's 13 and a half years old — he should be very cynical about life, he should be very bitter," he says. "But this is what he has to say." Before her death, the boy recounts, his mother told him: "I need you to be a good person in the society, like a doctor or engineer, and do good to the others." And, the boy adds, "despite everything that happened to my family, I will do anything that is good for the society.""If he can remind us about this," Justice Muralidhar says, "we have no excuse in not acting on the findings of this report."

title: NBA Free Agency: Celtics' Reported Reunion Target Makes Decision
description: It turns out that there won't be a reunion between the Boston Celtics and the 28-year-old center, who reportedly made his NBA free agency decision on Tuesday.
content:
NBA Free Agency: Celtics' Reported Reunion Target Makes Decision originally appeared on NESN.
                Add NESN as a Preferred Source by clicking here.It turns out that there won't be a reunion between the Boston Celtics and center Robert Williams III."Portland Trail Blazers center Robert Williams III has agreed to sign a three-year, $44 million contract extension to return to the Portland Trail Blazers through 2028-29, agent Kevin Bradbury of LIFT Sports Management tells ESPN," ESPN senior NBA insider Shams Charania posted on X.Portland Trail Blazers center Robert Williams III has agreed to sign a three-year, $44 million contract extension to return to the Portland Trail Blazers through 2028-29, agent Kevin Bradbury of LIFT Sports Management tells ESPN. pic.twitter.com/HKVhuQkojM— Shams Charania (@ShamsCharania) June 30, 2026It was reported multiple times recently that the Celtics were being linked to Williams, who was the team's first-round draft pick in 2018 and spent five seasons in Boston.The "Time Lord" averaged 7.3 points, 6.9 rebounds and 1.7 blocks per game across 209 regular season contests (97 starts) with the Celtics before being traded to the Portland Trail Blazers in October 2023. Williams earned second team All-Defense honors in 2022 with Boston and finished seventh in the NBA's Defensive Player of the Year voting (an award won by Marcus Smart) that season as well.The 28-year-old big man has been limited to 85 regular season games in three campaigns with the Trail Blazers thus far.Williams finished the 2025-26 season averaging 6.7 points, 7.0 rebounds, 1.5 blocks and 17.1 minutes per contest across 59 regular season games.More NBA: Celtics Legend, Hall-Of-Famer Finds Possibility Of Jaylen Brown Trade 'Disturbing'

title: Hardik Pandya moves base from Mumbai to Bengaluru: Here's why
description: Indian all-rounder Hardik Pandya has made a significant career shift, establishing his permanent training base at the BCCI's Centre of Excellence in Bengaluru. This move, driven by convenience and access to world-class facilities, marks him as the first high-profile active cricketer to do so. Pandya aims to continue playing international white-ball cricket for several more years, with his rehabilitation from injury ongoing.
content: India all-rounder Hardik Pandya has relocated to Bengaluru in a significant career move, choosing to make the BCCI's Centre of Excellence (COE) his primary training base for the foreseeable future. The decision makes Pandya the first active, high-profile India cricketer to permanently base himself near the COE, a facility that has traditionally been used by centrally contracted players for injury rehabilitation, fitness testing and national team camps rather than as a full-time training hub. Pandya, who hails from Baroda in Gujarat, had spent much of the last decade living in Mumbai, where he primarily trained at Mumbai Indians' facility in Ghansoli. However, the 32-year-old has now shifted to Bengaluru after spending a considerable amount of time at the COE over the past six months while recovering from a quadriceps injury. The injury ruled him out of India's ongoing white-ball tour of the UK, and he continues to undergo rehabilitation. "Hardik has already permanently shifted to Bengaluru. He has rented a property on the outskirts of the city, close to the COE. He will be the first Indian cricketer to make the COE his permanent training base for the remainder of his career," a BCCI source told PTI. According to the source, the move was driven by both convenience and access to world-class facilities. "Hardik wanted to move out of Mumbai as commuting every day from his Lower Parel residence for training had become a problem. As a centrally-contracted cricketer, he has access to every facility at the COE, from injury management to skills training. "Hence, he took the decision to make the COE his permanent base whenever he is not on IPL, state or national duty," the source said. Pandya is also expected to have a dedicated support team in Bengaluru, including his own physiotherapist and a personal strength and conditioning (S&C) coach, to oversee his training outside the COE. The source added that the India vice-captain intends to continue playing international white-ball cricket for several more years. "It is like shifting his base to Bengaluru as long as he plays white-ball cricket for India, and he intends to play for at least another five to six years. "Even when he does skill work, such as batting against net bowlers hired by the COE, Hardik pays them from his own pocket," the source added. Pandya's rehabilitation programme remains in progress. He recently took a short break for personal commitments and is expected to return to the Centre of Excellence within the next couple of days to resume his recovery. His availability for India's three-match T20I series against Zimbabwe, which follows the UK tour, remains uncertain. A decision on his selection will depend on whether he successfully completes the BCCI's Return-to-Play protocols and is declared fully fit.

title: LeBron James tells Lakers he plans to play elsewhere during next NBA season
description: LeBron James will continue his NBA career during the 2026-27 season but has informed the Lakers that the franchise can move on without him.
content: LeBron James will continue his NBA career during the 2026-27 season but has informed the Lakers that the franchise can move on without him because he will play elsewhere, ESPN reported Tuesday.ESPN reporter Shams Charania was first to report the development, citing Klutch Sports CEO Rich Paul.James will enter free agency to join a new team, according to ESPN. Sources told Charania on Monday that the Golden State Warriors were planning to pursue James once the free agency negotiating window begins Tuesday afternoon.After eight seasons with the Lakers, including leading the franchise to the 2020 NBA championship, James departs and enters free agency set to join a new team. Lakers president Rob Pelinka and Rich Paul spoke a short time ago about the four-time champion's plans, sources said.… https://t.co/QPwbkJi9OJ— Shams Charania (@ShamsCharania) June 30, 2026Draymond Green declined his $27.6 million player option Monday, opening up the flexibility for the Warriors to pursue James and a possible trade with the Washington Wizards for Anthony Davis, which would reunite the former Lakers teammates with Golden State.The 41-year-old James could have returned for a ninth season with the Lakers or retired as arguably the greatest basketball player of all time. Instead, he will choose a new team -- likely the last of his storied career."LeBron James is one of the greatest athletes in history," the Lakers' top executive and co-owner said in a statement. "We will always be thankful for his eight years with the Lakers -- including the title he led us to in 2020 under the toughest imaginable circumstances and the countless records he broke in purple and gold. We wish him all the best in the future, both on the court and off. He will always be a cherished part of the Lakers family." The statement was published on the Lakers' official social media accounts with the caption "Thank you, LeBron."James, who will turn 42 in December, saw his record 21-year All-NBA streak snapped last season as he played in just 60 games after missing the first month because of sciatica affecting his lower back and down his right leg.The four-time champion and four-time MVP will be entering what many consider to be the final chapter of his first-ballot Hall of Fame career while maintaining a level of performance unheard of for any other aging player.James was an All-Star last season, averaging 20.9 points per game on 51.5%, 7.2 assists and 6.1 rebounds. He was equally effective in the postseason, upping his playing time from 33.2 to 38.4 minutes per game and averaging 23.2 points, 7.3 assists and 6.7 rebounds. He led L.A. to a first-round series win over the Houston Rockets while Luka Doncic was sidelined with a hamstring strain.Thank you, LeBron 💜💛 pic.twitter.com/SW910ZVqSu— Los Angeles Lakers (@Lakers) June 30, 2026ESPN contributed to this report.
